source_notes.title: intellectronica/ruler — Apply the same rules to all coding agents
source_notes.excerpt: Centralised Rule Management... Automatic Distribution to configuration files of supported AI agents (GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor, OpenAI Codex CLI, Aider, Windsurf...). Rule Files (*.md) discovered recursively from .ruler/ and concatenated. License MIT, ~2.7k stars, v0.3.42 (May 2026).

source_notes.title: Ruler README — rule processing mechanism
source_notes.excerpt: Rule Files (*.md): Discovered recursively from .ruler/ and concatenated in order... Each file's content is prepended with <!-- Source: <relative_path_to_md_file> -->. No token templating or variable substitution; no prompt-injection defenses or input sanitization documented.

source_notes.title: dyoshikawa/rulesync — A Utility CLI for AI Coding Agents
source_notes.excerpt: Node.js CLI tool that generates configuration files for AI development tools from unified rule files... supports 30+ agents (Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI...). Unified syntax for rules, ignore files, MCP, commands, subagents, skills. v8.23.0 (May 2026), 242 releases, MIT, ~1.1k stars.

source_notes.title: Goldziher/ai-rulez — Complete AI development workflow for 19+ tools
source_notes.excerpt: Write your rules, context, skills, agents, and commands once in .ai-rulez/ then generate native configs for Claude, Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Gemini, Codex and more. 19+ platforms. Written in Go. JSON schema directory present. v4.3.2 (May 2026), MIT.

source_notes.title: Agent RuleZ: A Deterministic Policy Engine for AI Coding Agents
source_notes.excerpt: Practitioner write-up framing ai-rulez-class tooling as a 'deterministic policy engine' for AI coding agents — signalling that deterministic generation and validation are already a marketed differentiator in the multi-target-emit space, not unique to one tool.

source_notes.title: AGENTS.md — a simple, open format for guiding coding agents
source_notes.excerpt: A simple, open format for guiding coding agents... just standard Markdown. No JSON schema, no YAML, no special syntax. Stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation. Over 60,000 open-source projects use it. No templating, build resolution, or security validation.

source_notes.title: agentsmd/agents.md GitHub repository
source_notes.excerpt: A simple, open format for guiding coding agents... a README for agents. 21.9k stars, 1.6k forks. No multi-target emit, templating, or security validation in the convention itself; it is a single-file Markdown convention agents read natively.

source_notes.title: Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills
source_notes.excerpt: We've published Agent Skills as an open standard for cross-platform portability (Dec 18, 2025 update)... organized folders of instructions, scripts, and resources agents discover and load dynamically. A single portable format, not author-once-emit-to-many. Security addressed only via 'install from trusted sources' + manual audit.

source_notes.title: anthropics/skills — Public repository for Agent Skills
source_notes.excerpt: Skills are a folder with a SKILL.md file containing YAML frontmatter (required: name, description) and instructions. Format released as open standard Dec 2025; OpenAI adopted the same format for Codex CLI and ChatGPT. No build-time token resolution or input security validation in the spec.

source_notes.title: block/ai-rules — Manage AI rules, commands, and skills across multiple coding agents
source_notes.excerpt: Manage AI rules, commands, and skills across multiple coding agents from one place — a corporate-backed (Block) entrant confirming the author-once/multi-target-emit category is being commoditised by vendors beyond solo maintainers.

source_notes.title: Prompt injection — unsolved LLM weakness (security context)
source_notes.excerpt: Prompt injection is a fundamental, unsolved weakness: LLMs process trusted instructions and untrusted data through the same token stream with no architectural separation. Build-time concatenation tools do not address the runtime injection vector.

claims.statement: Ruler already provides author-once -> multi-target emit across Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code and Codex, deterministically at apply-time, as a mature MIT-licensed CLI.

evidence.quote: Automatic Distribution... applying rules to configuration files of supported AI agents (GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, Cursor, OpenAI Codex CLI...). v0.3.42 (May 2026), MIT, ~2.7k stars.
Rule Files (*.md): Discovered recursively from .ruler/ and concatenated in order... ruler apply distributes to each agent's native format.

claims.falsifier: Evidence that ruler cannot emit to one of Copilot/Cursor/Claude Code/Codex, or that the project is unmaintained (no release since 2025).

claims.statement: rulesync independently commoditises the same author-once -> multi-target generation across 30+ agents including Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code and Codex, with import/export and high release cadence.

evidence.quote: Node.js CLI tool that generates configuration files for AI development tools from unified rule files... supports 30+ agents... 242 releases, v8.23.0 (May 2026), MIT.
Write your rules... once in .ai-rulez/ then generate native configs for Claude, Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Gemini, Codex and more.

claims.falsifier: Evidence rulesync's generate command does not actually produce per-agent native configs, or that it dropped Codex/Copilot support.

claims.statement: Author-once -> multi-target agent-artefact emit is a commoditised category with at least three independent maintainers (ruler, rulesync, ai-rulez) plus a corporate entrant (block/ai-rules), not a novel capability.

evidence.quote: Complete AI development workflow for 19+ tools... generate native configs for Claude, Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Gemini, Codex and more.
Manage AI rules, commands, and skills across multiple coding agents from one place.

claims.falsifier: Demonstration that all named tools are forks of one codebase, or that none can actually emit to multiple targets simultaneously.

claims.statement: Ruler performs only concatenation with source-marker prepending; it does NOT support {{token}} templating or variable substitution.

evidence.quote: Ruler does not support token templating, variable substitution, or placeholders... operates through straightforward concatenation. Each file's content is prepended with <!-- Source: ... -->.
Rule Files (*.md) discovered recursively from .ruler/ and concatenated.

claims.falsifier: A ruler.toml or docs section showing variable/placeholder interpolation in rule bodies.

claims.statement: No surveyed commodity tool's primary docs confirm {{token}} build-time templating of rule content; ai-rulez ships a JSON schema and profiles/presets but does not document variable substitution.

evidence.quote: 33 builtin domains... content organization via domains/profiles, remote includes... JSON schema directory present — but README does not document template syntax or variable substitution.
No token templating or variable substitution... distributes Markdown verbatim.

claims.falsifier: ai-rulez/rulesync source or docs showing a real variable-interpolation engine ({{var}}, includes-with-substitution); ai-rulez 'remote includes' may partly satisfy this and would weaken the gap.

claims.statement: None of the surveyed commodity multi-target tools perform build-time SECURITY validation of rule inputs (prompt-injection / supply-chain scanning); their 'security' is limited to backups, gitignore, secret-scan configs, or 'trust the author' assumptions.

evidence.quote: The documentation does not address prompt injection defenses or input sanitization... distributes Markdown verbatim without filtering, validation, or security preprocessing.
Security addressed only via 'install from trusted sources' and advising manual auditing before use.

claims.falsifier: Documentation showing ruler/rulesync/ai-rulez actively scan rule content for injection/supply-chain attacks at build time and fail the build on violation.

claims.statement: Deterministic / policy-engine generation is itself becoming a marketed feature of commodity tools, so 'deterministic build' alone is not a defensible differentiator for agent-enterprise.

evidence.quote: Agent RuleZ: A Deterministic Policy Engine for AI Coding Agents — framing this tool class as a deterministic policy engine.
Built-in security auditor agent; supports input validation rules and secrets handling standards within governance framework.

claims.falsifier: Evidence that ai-rulez's 'deterministic' and 'security auditor' claims are runtime-agent features, not build-time guarantees — which would re-open determinism as a differentiator.

claims.statement: AGENTS.md is a Linux-Foundation-stewarded single-file Markdown CONVENTION read natively by major agents; it is NOT an author-once-emit-to-many build system and adds no templating or security validation, so convergence on it shrinks the need for any emitter.

evidence.quote: A simple, open format... just standard Markdown. No JSON schema, no YAML, no special syntax. Stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation. Over 60,000 projects.
A README for agents... 21.9k stars. No multi-target emit, templating, or security validation in the convention.

claims.falsifier: Evidence AGENTS.md spec adds a resolution/templating layer or that agents stopped reading it natively, re-creating the need for per-agent emit.

claims.statement: Anthropic Agent Skills is an open cross-platform standard (adopted by OpenAI Codex/ChatGPT) using a single portable SKILL.md format — solving cross-agent portability by convergence rather than by build-time fan-out, with no templating/security-validation layer.

evidence.quote: We've published Agent Skills as an open standard for cross-platform portability (Dec 18, 2025).
Format released as open standard Dec 2025; OpenAI adopted the same format for Codex CLI and ChatGPT.

claims.falsifier: Evidence OpenAI/others did NOT adopt the Skills format, or that Skills require per-platform recompilation rather than being read natively.

claims.statement: agent-enterprise's only genuinely differentiated substrate elements versus 2026 commodity tooling are (a) {{token}} build-time templating combined with (b) build-time security validation of inputs (injection/supply-chain) — the raw multi-target emit is redundant.

evidence.quote: No token templating... no prompt-injection defenses or input sanitization documented.
LLMs process trusted instructions and untrusted data through the same token stream with no architectural separation — build-time concatenation tools do not address this.

claims.falsifier: ai-rulez 'security auditor' / 'input validation rules' or rulesync secretlint integration turn out to deliver equivalent build-time injection/supply-chain validation, collapsing the residual novelty to near-zero.
